INR achieves success in parameterizing various signals such as 3D scenes, images, audio, and videos by representing them as a function approximated by a neural network.

NeRV is an INR-based method that is favorable compared to learning-based video compression techniques for simpler training pipelines and faster video decoding speed.
Existing INR-based methods are limited to encoding a single short video at a time.
A straightforward strategy for encoding diverse videos is to divide them into multiple subsets and model each of them by a separate neural network.

D-NeRV can represent different videos in a single model by conditioning on key-frames, while NeRV encodes each video into a separate model.

Temporal reasoning is introduced into the INR-based network by explicitly modeling global temporal dependencies across different frames.

node_2: shared implicit neural network based decoder
edge: Videos of the same action type can share similar motion information, modeled by a shared implicit neural network based decoder.
The motion-aware decoder provides motion information to reconstruct the full video with visual content from key-frames.
The motion-aware decoder is different from the standard implicit neural network as it takes in both the time coordinates and the content feature map.
The motion-aware decoder takes in both the time coordinates and the content feature map.
The motion-aware decoder takes in both the time coordinates and the content feature map.
The motion-aware decoder predicts task-oriented flows as intermediate output.
The task-oriented flows are used to warp the generated content features.
A spatially-adaptive fusion module is proposed to fuse the content information into the decoder in a more effective manner.
The decoder is equipped with temporal modeling ability by the proposed global temporal MLP module.
The multi-scale Flow Estimation network is used for predicting the task-oriented flow at each time step.
Given two content feature maps, linear interpolation is applied along the time axis to generate the feature map at every intermediate time.
Following NeRV, the input time index is mapped by the positional encoding function into a higher dimensional embedding space.
The input of the flow estimation module is the feature map concatenated with the positional encoding before being fed into the flow estimation module.
The forward flow Ft→0 and backward flow Ft→1 at each time t ∈ [0, 1] are computed simultaneously.
Frames are related to key-frames as the process involves propagating visual content from key-frames to the current frame.
The flow estimation module takes a feature map M lt as input, which is generated from the previous decoder stage.
The stack of convolutions in the flow estimation module calculates the per-pixel flow for generating high-quality content feature maps.
Content feature maps are strategically propagated for each timestep in the process of estimating flows and generating high-quality feature maps.
Forward and backward warped feature maps are generated at each timestep using the bilinear warp operation.
The distance-aware confidence score is used to weight sum the warped features for generating the fused warping feature.
The warped feature map contains clip-specific content information for each timestep, used in the spatially-adaptive fusion module.
The motion-aware decoder includes a spatially-adaptive fusion module to fuse clip-specific content information, inspired by the success of modulation layers.
The fusion module is designed to fuse the clip-specific content information.
The fusion module is motivated by the recent success of modulation layers.
Modulation layers help in learning pixel-wise modulation parameters.
Pixel-wise modulation parameters are learned by passing the content feature.
The content feature integrates two feature maps effectively.
After modulation operation, the block architecture of NeRV consists of specific layers for feature map up-sampling.
NeRV takes the time index as input and outputs the corresponding frame directly.
Inspired by the success of attention-based transformers and MLP-based models, a global temporal MLP module is introduced.
The global temporal MLP module is proposed as a way to model temporal relationships across different frames, motivated by the use of highly optimized fully-connected layers.
The global temporal MLP module uses the feature map of T frames to model global temporal dependencies through fully connected layers.
The fully connected layer with specific weights is applied along the time axis to model global temporal dependencies in the feature map.
The decoder feature map and the warped frame are concatenated and fed into convolution layers for final refinement.
Both the reconstructed frame and the ground-truth frame are optimized using a combination of L1 and SSIM loss during training.
